data: Welcome to this series of articles dedicated to Software-Defined Radio (SDR). SDR is a technology that is transforming the way radio systems are designed and deployed. Traditionally, radios were built with dedicated hardware components for each signal-processing function (amplification, modulation, demodulation, etc.). By contrast, in an SDR, much of this processing is carried out by software, enabling greater flexibility, reusability, and adaptability.
data.commercial-and-military-growth-2000s#Com:
In the 2000s, SDR increasingly became a reality in both commercial and military applications. The military, in particular, recognized SDR as a way to reduce the complexity and costs of communication systems. It allowed rapid adaptation of communication frequencies and protocols without replacing hardware—especially valuable in combat environments where flexibility is critical.
In 2000, companies such as National Instruments and Ettus Research began developing accessible SDR platforms, such as the NI-USRP (Universal Software Radio Peripheral) and FlexRadio Systems, facilitating SDR implementations across diverse contexts—from wireless networks to academic research and radio signal monitoring.
data.conclusion#Conclusion: The history of software-defined radio is defined by a series of breakthroughs in digital signal processing. From early research in the 1960s to the open-source democratization of the 2010s, SDR has radically transformed the way communication systems are designed and used. Thanks to pioneers like Joseph Mitola and its initial military implementations, SDR has now become a cornerstone of modern telecommunications.
data.early-explorations-1960s1970s#Early Expl:
The concept of software-defined radio dates back to the 1960s–1970s, but it was only with the rise of computers and digital technologies that the idea truly began to take shape. The notion of a “programmable” or adaptable radio emerged when researchers started exploring ways to process signals using software.
At that time, most radios were primarily analog. The need for more flexible radios, capable of handling different types of signals, was evident in both telecommunications and the military, where systems needed to adapt to various modulation schemes and frequency ranges.
data.series-outline-exploring-software-define: Introduction to Software-Defined Radio Theoretical Basics of Radio Analog and Heterodyne Architectures Signal Digitization Technology SDR Tools SDR Architecture Applications of Software-Defined Radio SDR Security The Future of Software-Defined Radio Summary of Concepts Let’s dive into the world of software-defined radios.
data.the-birth-of-modern-sdr-1980s1990s#The B:
In the 1980s, advances in Digital Signal Processors (DSPs) enabled more sophisticated digital signal processing, laying the groundwork for modern SDR. Research intensified around digital modulation, with scientists exploring the possibility of replacing certain hardware components with software—especially for radio signal processing.
The term Software-Defined Radio was introduced in 1984 by Joseph Mitola. In 1992, he published a seminal paper describing SDR as a radio that uses software to perform signal-processing functions previously handled by hardware components.
data.the-era-of-democratization-and-open-sour:
The 2010s marked the true democratization of SDR. Open-source platforms such as GNU Radio, SDR# (SDRSharp), and HDSDR made SDR technology accessible to a wide audience, from hobbyists to researchers. These tools allowed users to build their own SDR setups at relatively low cost, transforming how scientists, engineers, and enthusiasts approach radio technology.
The emergence of devices like the Raspberry Pi and affordable hardware components such as the RTL-SDR made entry into the SDR world highly cost-effective, opening the door to a new generation of amateur radio operators and innovators.

data: In this section, we will explore the fundamental concepts of radio, essential for understanding how Software-Defined Radios (SDR) work. We will cover key notions such as baseband, modulation, and channel capacity. These concepts will form the foundation for understanding the technical challenges that SDRs address.
data.article-outline#Article Outline: Baseband Modulation Channel Capacity (Shannon) Noise Baseband Filtering in SDR
data.baseband#Baseband:
A baseband signal refers to the original signal as produced by the source, before any transformation for transmission. For example, a digital audio file is a baseband signal: its spectrum is centered around zero frequency and extends to a maximum frequency related to its content (a few kHz for voice). Modulation
To transmit this signal over a physical medium (cable, fiber, radio waves), it is often necessary to modulate it. Modulation consists of “carrying” the information on a higher-frequency carrier wave in order to:
Adapt the signal to the transmission channel, Enable simultaneous transmission of multiple signals (multiplexing), Reduce interference in certain media. There are several types of modulation:
Amplitude Modulation (AM) – the carrier’s amplitude varies according to the signal being transmitted. Frequency Modulation (FM) – the carrier’s frequency varies with the signal. Phase Modulation (PM) – the carrier’s phase changes to encode the information.
data.baseband-filtering#Baseband Filtering:
Filtering a baseband signal is essential in communication systems for several reasons. Primarily, it helps reduce inter-symbol interference. Filtering also limits the signal’s bandwidth, preventing spectral waste and ensuring compliance with frequency regulations.
Baseband filtering is therefore a key concept in software-defined radio. Common filter types include:
Gaussian filters Raised cosine filters Matched filters Each filter type offers specific advantages and challenges depending on the application context.
Whereas in traditional radio systems filtering is done using analog components, in SDR it is implemented digitally. This offers greater flexibility, since the filter’s characteristics can be changed simply through software programming, without the need to modify hardware.
data.noise#Noise:
Noise is any unwanted disturbance that corrupts a radio signal. It can come from natural sources (such as thermal noise from heat in electronic circuits) or from artificial sources (such as interference from other devices—for example, a microwave near a Wi-Fi router).
As we saw earlier, noise limits a channel’s capacity by making signals harder to decode.
In traditional systems, noise can be mitigated with amplifiers and analog components. In SDR systems, noise is often reduced using digital algorithms that dynamically adapt signal processing. This makes it possible to maintain optimal signal quality in noisy environments.
data.shannon-and-channel-capacity#Shannon and:
In 1948, Claude Elwood Shannon modeled, for the first time, a communication channel in his paper A Mathematical Theory of Communication. This model describes all the components involved in transmitting a signal, from the information source to its destination.
Schéma d'un canal de communication Shannon was a pioneer in information theory. He established a fundamental theorem that defines the capacity of a channel.
The formula modeling this is:
C=BWlog⁡2(1+SN) C = BW \log_{2}{(1+\frac{S}{N})} C=BWlog2​(1+NS​) C – Channel capacity (bits/s) BW – Bandwidth (Hz) S/N – Signal-to-noise ratio (dimensionless, linear scale) Shannon’s formula calculates the maximum capacity of a channel—that is, the maximum amount of information it can transmit per second. The capacity depends on two key factors:
The bandwidth The signal-to-noise ratio In practice, this formula helps us understand how to maximize information transfer in systems such as wireless networks.
** Example ** – Theoretical Capacity vs. Actual Throughput (Wi-Fi case)
Let’s consider a Wi-Fi network:
Available bandwidth: B=20 MHzB=20MHz Measured signal-to-noise ratio: S/N=25 dBS/N=25dB Convert the S/N ratio: SN=102510=102.5≈316,23 \frac{S}{N} = 10^{\frac{25}{10}} = 10^{2.5} \approx 316,23 NS​=101025​=102.5≈316,23 Apply Shannon’s formula: C=BWlog⁡2(1+SN) C = BW \log_{2}{(1+\frac{S}{N})} C=BWlog2​(1+NS​) C=20⋅106log⁡2(1+316,23) C = 20 \cdot 10^{6} \log_{2}{(1+316,23)} C=20⋅106log2​(1+316,23) C≈20⋅106⋅8,31≈166.2Mbit/s C \approx 20 \cdot 10^{6} \cdot 8,31 \approx 166.2 Mbit/s C≈20⋅106⋅8,31≈166.2Mbit/sThe theoretical capacity is about 166 Mb/s, whereas the actual throughput is often between 80 Mb/s and 120 Mb/s. This difference is due to losses, interference, protocol overhead, and collisions. In practice, the real throughput is always lower than the maximum channel capacity.

data: When we think of radio communication, we often picture an antenna picking up invisible waves carrying music, conversations, or data. But behind this image lies an entire chain of electronics. In this article, we’ll explore one of the most widely used architectures in radio signal processing: the heterodyne.
data.advantages-of-the-heterodyne-architectur: This architecture offers several advantages. First, it provides excellent selectivity thanks to precise filtering at the intermediate frequency, making it easy to choose the desired station or channel while eliminating unwanted signals. It also improves receiver sensitivity, enabling the reception of very weak signals. In addition, it is highly flexible: simply changing the local oscillator frequency allows coverage of different radio bands without altering the entire circuit. Finally, it offers better operational stability, as the circuits operate at a lower frequency than the original RF, reducing noise and distortion.
data.article-outline#Article Outline: Basic Components of an RF Chain The Principle of the Heterodyne in Radio Key Components in a Heterodyne Architecture Advantages of the Heterodyne Architecture
data.basic-components-of-an-rf-chain#Basic Co:
An RF (radio frequency) chain is made up of components that work together to receive, amplify, filter, convert, and transmit a radio signal. Everything starts with the antenna, which captures or emits electromagnetic waves. The received signal then passes through an RF filter, which selects only the desired frequency band and removes unwanted signals.
A low-noise amplifier (LNA) then boosts this weak signal while preserving its quality. The mixer comes next, combining the RF signal with a reference signal provided by a local oscillator, allowing the signal’s frequency to be shifted.
On the transmit side, a power amplifier boosts the signal before sending it to the antenna. Finally, when signals need to be processed digitally, analog-to-digital and digital-to-analog converters handle the transition between the analog and digital domains.
Schéma d'une chaine RF en émission
data.specific-components-in-a-heterodyne-arch:
In a heterodyne architecture, the signal follows a specific path: After being captured by the antenna and cleaned by the RF filter, the signal is amplified by the LNA. It is then sent to the mixer, which uses the local oscillator’s frequency to convert it into an intermediate frequency.
This IF is then passed through a dedicated IF filter, which precisely isolates the desired band, and then through an IF amplifier to boost its level. Finally, a demodulator extracts the useful information.
data.the-principle-of-the-heterodyne-in-radio:
The heterodyne method, invented in the early 20th century, simplifies radio signal processing. Instead of working directly with the original signal frequency—often very high—it is mixed with a signal generated by a local oscillator to produce a lower intermediate frequency (IF).
This conversion does not alter the information contained in the signal, but shifts it to a frequency that is easier to amplify and filter.
For example, if a receiver picks up a signal at 100 MHz and the local oscillator generates a 90 MHz signal, the mixer will produce, among other things, a 10 MHz signal. This intermediate frequency, much lower than the original RF, allows the use of simpler and more efficient circuits.
data.the-principle-of-the-heterodyne-mixer#Th:
The mixer is a key component in a heterodyne chain. It performs frequency conversion—in electronics, “mixing” means multiplying two signals rather than blending them.
If the RF signal captured by the antenna is:
sRF(t)=ARF⋅cos⁡(2πfRFt) s_{RF} (t) = A_{RF} \cdot \cos(2\pi f_{RF}t) sRF​(t)=ARF​⋅cos(2πfRF​t)and the local oscillator produces:
sLO(t)=ALO⋅cos⁡(2πfLOt) s_{LO} (t) = A_{LO} \cdot \cos(2\pi f_{LO}t) sLO​(t)=ALO​⋅cos(2πfLO​t)then the mixer performs the multiplication:
sout(t)=sRF(t)⋅sLO(t) s_{out} (t) = s_{RF} (t) \cdot s_{LO} (t) sout​(t)=sRF​(t)⋅sLO​(t)Using the trigonometric identity:
cos⁡(A)⋅cos⁡(B)=12[cos⁡(A+B)+cos⁡(A−B)] \cos(A) \cdot \cos(B) = \frac{1}{2} [\cos(A+B) + \cos(A-B)] cos(A)⋅cos(B)=21​[cos(A+B)+cos(A−B)]we get:
sout=ARFALO2[cos(2π(fRF+fLO)t)+cos(2π(fRF−fLO)t)] s_{out} = \frac{A_{RF} A_{LO}}{2} [cos (2\pi (f_{RF}+f_{LO})t) + cos (2\pi (f_{RF}-f_{LO})t)] sout​=2ARF​ALO​​[cos(2π(fRF​+fLO​)t)+cos(2π(fRF​−fLO​)t)]This means the mixer produces two new frequencies:
One at the sum of the input frequencies One at the difference In practice, an RF filter selects the difference component, called the intermediate frequency (IF):
fFI=∣fRF−fLO∣ f_{FI} = |f_{RF} - f_{LO}| fFI​=∣fRF​−fLO​∣This IF is then amplified and processed, since it is much easier to handle than the direct RF signal.

data: Software-defined radio is built on a simple principle: replacing most analog RF and IF processing stages with digital operations. The transition point between these two worlds is signal digitization. This is the precise moment when an analog signal, captured by the antenna and shaped by the RF front-end, becomes a sequence of samples that can be processed by algorithms. The performance of an SDR is therefore directly linked to the quality of this analog-to-digital conversion.
data.acquisition-architectures-in-sdr#Acquisi:
The choice of acquisition architecture determines the complexity of the front-end and the constraints placed on the ADC.
The heterodyne approach remains very common: the RF signal is first shifted to a lower intermediate frequency using a mixer, then filtered before digitization. This method reduces the required sampling frequency and allows the use of less expensive ADCs that are less sensitive to clock jitter. However, it requires multiple analog stages, which come with their own drawbacks: intermodulation distortion, local oscillator phase noise, thermal drift, and the need for regular calibration.
At the opposite end, direct sampling feeds the ADC directly with the RF band. This approach eliminates the intermediate frequency and greatly simplifies the analog chain, while offering a very wide instantaneous bandwidth. However, it demands extremely fast converters—often operating at several gigasamples per second—whose cost, power consumption, and sensitivity to jitter are significantly higher.
Between these two extremes, bandpass sampling occupies a special place. It relies on the deliberate spectral folding of an RF band into the Nyquist zone by precisely choosing the sampling frequency. This makes it possible to process high-frequency signals using a slower ADC, but requires very selective input filtering to prevent undesirable overlap from other bands.
data.article-outline#Article Outline: Digitization: Recap and Deep Dive Acquisition Architectures in SDR Critical Parameters
data.critical-parameters#Critical Parameters:
Merely specifying the sampling frequency and number of bits is not enough to describe the actual performance of an SDR chain. Other parameters directly influence the quality of the digitized signal.
ENOB (Effective Number of Bits) expresses the effective resolution while accounting for the noise and distortion generated by the converter. It is always lower than the theoretical resolution and tends to decrease as the input frequency increases.
SFDR (Spurious-Free Dynamic Range) measures the system’s ability to detect a weak signal in the presence of strong interfering signals. In crowded RF environments, a high SFDR is crucial to prevent intermodulation products from masking signals of interest.
The sampling clock jitter also plays a major role, especially at high frequencies. For example, an RMS jitter of 100 femtoseconds limits the SNR to about 70 dB for a 100 MHz signal—and considerably less for a signal in the gigahertz range.
Finally, total latency—the delay between RF capture and the availability of processed samples—can become critical in applications such as radar, real-time bidirectional communications, or adaptive control systems.
The overall dynamic range, which combines ADC resolution, SFDR, and automatic gain control, determines the system’s ability to handle signals of widely varying amplitudes without distortion or saturation.
data.digitization-recap-and-deep-dive#Digitiz:
Analog-to-digital conversion first relies on sampling, i.e., measuring the signal’s amplitude at regular intervals defined by a sampling frequency fefe​.
The Nyquist–Shannon theorem requires that fefe​ be at least twice the maximum frequency contained in the signal; otherwise, irreversible spectral folding known as aliasing will corrupt the information.
In a radio context, it is sometimes possible to circumvent the apparent need for ultra-high sampling rates by using undersampling or bandpass sampling. This technique targets a narrow RF band—even at high frequency—and chooses a sampling frequency that intentionally folds this band into the Nyquist zone, provided the anti-alias filtering is perfectly controlled.
Once the sampling instants are defined, the ADC performs quantization, assigning a digital value encoded over NN bits to each measurement. The resolution obtained determines the theoretical signal-to-noise ratio due to quantization, given by:
SNRquant=6.02N+1.76dB SNR_{quant} = 6.02N+1.76 dB SNRquant​=6.02N+1.76dBEach additional bit doubles the precision and increases the SNR by about 6 dB, but also generates a larger data volume. The raw data rate is equal to the product of fefe​ by NN.
For example, a stream of 250 mega-samples per second at 14 bits already represents 3.5 gigabits per second, requiring the downstream FPGA or DSP to have sufficient internal bandwidth to process the data in real time.

/website/blog/2025-08-09-sdr4/.title: Signal Digitization Technology: The Heart of Software-Defined Radio

data: Software-defined radio (SDR) owes its flexibility to the fact that most modulation, demodulation, and filtering operations are performed digitally. But to efficiently handle an RF signal in the digital domain, a representation that preserves the full amplitude and phase information is required. This is precisely the role of the IQ architecture: to provide the processor with a complex signal composed of an In-phase (I) component and a Quadrature (Q) component, perfectly synchronized and shifted by 90°. This representation, inherited from complex signal theory, has become indispensable in SDR.
data.advantages-of-the-iq-representation#Adva: Using a complex baseband signal allows both the positive and negative components of the spectrum to be represented unambiguously, effectively doubling the usable bandwidth compared to a real signal sampled at the same rate. Complex modulations such as QPSK, QAM, OFDM, or any vector modulation are naturally expressed in this formalism. Furthermore, frequency shifts can be implemented simply through complex multiplication, eliminating the need for additional analog operations and making SDR systems inherently more flexible.
data.article-outline#Article Outline: Origin and principle of the IQ representation Hardware implementation of the IQ architecture Advantages of the IQ representation Limitations and real-world imperfections The role of IQ in the SDR signal chain
data.conclusion#Conclusion: The IQ architecture is far more than a simple representation convention—it is a prerequisite for fully leveraging the power of digital processing in SDR. By simultaneously providing both the amplitude and phase of the signal, it enables direct manipulation of complex spectra, implementation of advanced modulation schemes, and digital correction of hardware imperfections. Mastery of this architecture, both in theory and in practice, is an essential skill for any engineer involved in the design or operation of modern SDR systems.
data.hardware-implementation-of-the-iq-archit:
In reception, the generation of the IQ stream relies on a quadrature mixer. The incoming RF signal is first split into two identical paths. The first is multiplied by a cosine local oscillator (LO) signal. The second is multiplied by a sine LO signal, obtained by introducing an exact 90° phase shift to the LO. The mixing products contain both the sum and the difference of the input and LO frequencies; a low-pass filter retains only the intermediate frequency component near zero. Each filtered path is then digitized by an independent analog-to-digital converter (ADC), producing two parallel streams: I[n]I[n] and Q[n]Q[n].
In transmission, the process is symmetrical: the digital I and Q streams generated by the processor are fed into digital-to-analog converters (DACs), then recombined by a quadrature mixer to reconstruct a real RF signal.
data.limitations-and-real-world-imperfections: In practice, generating perfectly matched I and Q channels is impossible. Small amplitude imbalances between the two paths, phase errors relative to the ideal 90°, or nonlinearities introduce imperfect image rejection. These imperfections manifest as an unwanted mirror signal symmetric with respect to the center frequency. For example, a 2° phase error or a 0.5 dB amplitude imbalance can limit image rejection to less than 40 dB. In modern SDR systems, however, these defects can be mitigated through digital calibration algorithms, often executed automatically at startup or continuously during operation.
data.origin-and-principle-of-the-iq-represent:
A pure RF signal can be described by a sinusoid:
s(t)=Acos⁡(ωt+ϕ) s(t) = A \cos(\omega t + \phi)s(t)=Acos(ωt+ϕ)In practice, a modulated signal occupies a certain bandwidth around the carrier frequency ωω and contains variations in amplitude and phase. To process it efficiently, it is preferable to represent it as a combination of two orthogonal components:
s(t)=I(t)cos⁡(ωt)−Q(t)sin⁡(ωt) s(t) = I (t) \cos(\omega t) - Q(t) \sin (\omega t)s(t)=I(t)cos(ωt)−Q(t)sin(ωt)where I(t)I(t) and Q(t)Q(t) are two baseband signals carrying, respectively, the real and imaginary parts of a complex version of the signal. In this notation, the signal becomes:
sc=I(t)+jQ(t) s_{c} = I(t) + j Q(t)sc​=I(t)+jQ(t)This approach makes it possible to translate modulated signals into a complex baseband representation, where operations such as frequency shifting, filtering, and demodulation become mathematically straightforward.
data.role-of-iq-in-the-sdr-signal-chain#Role: In a direct-sampling SDR architecture, the ADC output may already provide an IQ stream if the conversion stage integrates IQ generation internally. In heterodyne architectures, IQ generation takes place after one or more intermediate frequency shifts but before purely digital processing. In all cases, it is this IQ stream that is delivered to the FPGA, DSP, or host processor for analysis, filtering, demodulation, or retransmission.

/website/blog/2025-08-09-sdr5/.title: IQ Architecture: Foundation of Digital Processing in SDR
